html:
Frankenstein — 10 Most Likely AQA Questions + Skeleton Essay Plans

For: AQA GCSE English Literature, Paper 1 Section B (the 19th-century novel). 30 marks. AO1 + AO2 + AO3 (no AO4 SPaG on this paper). ~50 minutes.

Source. The plans below distil the ideas, frames and analytical moves from Sophicly's 9 Grade-9 Frankenstein model answers (`Model Answers/AQA/Frankenstein/`). Each plan reuses the same load-bearing scenes, the same controlling concept, and the same reading-strategies students already know from the Frankenstein crib sheet.

How to use this document. Each entry below gives:

1. The AQA-style question.

2. The full typical extract.

3. The frame line (the controlling argument behind the plan).

4. A block of key technique terms with plain-English glosses.

5. A skeleton plan in the locked structure used across every Sophicly worked essay.

The locked structure:

• Introduction — 3 sentences (Hook + Building sentence + Thesis; ~110 words).

• Body Paragraph 1 — 7 sentences (Beginning of the novel; ~210–225 words).

• Body Paragraph 2 — 7 sentences (Middle of the novel; the printed extract usually sits here; ~225–250 words).

• Body Paragraph 3 — 7 sentences (End of the novel; ~225–250 words).

• Conclusion — 4 sentences (~110–155 words).

The seven-sentence body-paragraph shape:

1. Topic — pure concept, no techniques named yet.

2. Technique + evidence — ONE quote, with two rhetorical/linguistic techniques visible inside that quote.

3. Close analysis — the words inside the quote, unpacked. Grammar, connotation, word-class can sit here.

4. Effect on audience 1 — what the reader feels or thinks.

5. Effect on audience 2 — a different feeling or thought.

6. Author's purpose — what Shelley may be arguing.

7. Context (AO3) — one factor, woven in, never bolted on.

Each plan reuses the same load-bearing scenes — the Frankenstein corpus rests on Walton's Letters 1–4, Volume I Chapter 5 (the animation), Chapter 8 (Justine's hanging), Chapter 10 (Mer de Glace), Chapters 11–16 (the Creature's narrative), Chapter 20 (the destruction of the female creature), Chapter 23 (Elizabeth's wedding-night murder), and Chapter 24 (the Arctic deathbed).

---

Memorise these once

Controlling concept of Frankenstein. Creation without responsibility makes monsters. Sympathy is the moral instrument human institutions consistently fail to provide. The novel's three-frame structure trains the reader in exactly the moral attention its characters refuse — by the time the Creature speaks for himself, the reader cannot un-hear his eloquence, and Victor's earlier judgement of him has collapsed.

Key terms that recur in the plans below — gloss every one when you use it.

• The three-frame structure = Walton's letters (outer frame) → Victor's narrative inside Walton's letters (middle frame) → the Creature's narrative inside Victor's narrative (inner frame). The form trains the reader to feel sympathy where Victor refuses it.

• Hamartia = a fatal error in action (something the protagonist does), NOT a fatal flaw of character (something the protagonist is). Victor's hamartia is most precisely the moment of abandonment in Chapter 5 — not the act of creation itself.

• Bildungsroman = a novel of moral and intellectual formation; the German tradition (Goethe's Wilhelm Meister, 1795–96) of narrating a young protagonist's growing-up across years of testing experiences. The Creature's six chapters in Volume II are a compressed Bildungsroman.

• Romantic-period sublime = the experience of awe in the face of vast, terrifying nature (Mont Blanc, the Arctic) — used by Shelley to dwarf human ambition.

• Galvanism = the science of re-animating dead tissue using electricity, named after Luigi Galvani (1791 frog experiments) and developed by Giovanni Aldini (1803 corpse-reanimation experiment at Newgate, London).

• Promethean myth = the Greek myth of Prometheus, who stole fire from the gods to give to humanity and was punished eternally; the novel's subtitle is The Modern Prometheus.

When you write these terms in an essay, gloss each in brackets the first time you use it. AQA examiners reward the term + the gloss together.

---

Q1 — The Creature: more human than the humans who reject him?

Preamble

In this extract, the Creature recalls his earliest memories of awakening alone after Victor's abandonment.

Extract

Volume II Chapter 11 — the Creature's earliest memories of being abandoned by Victor:

""It is with considerable difficulty that I remember the original era of my being; all the events of that period appear confused and indistinct. A strange multiplicity of sensations seized me, and I saw, felt, heard, and smelt at the same time; and it was, indeed, a long time before I learned to distinguish between the operations of my various senses. By degrees, I remember, a stronger light pressed upon my nerves, so that I was obliged to shut my eyes. Darkness then came over me and troubled me, but hardly had I felt this when, by opening my eyes, as I now suppose, the light poured in upon me again. I walked and, I believe, descended, but I presently found a great alteration in my sensations. Before, dark and opaque bodies had surrounded me, impervious to my touch or sight; but I now found that I could wander on at liberty, with no obstacles which I could not either surmount or avoid. The light became more and more oppressive to me, and the heat wearying me as I walked, I sought a place where I could receive shade. This was the forest near Ingolstadt; and here I lay by the side of a brook resting from my fatigue, until I felt tormented by hunger and thirst. This roused me from my nearly dormant state, and I ate some berries which I found hanging on the trees or lying on the ground. I slaked my thirst at the brook, and then lying down, was overcome by sleep.

"It was dark when I awoke; I felt cold also, and half frightened, as it were, instinctively, finding myself so desolate. Before I had quitted your apartment, on a sensation of cold, I had covered myself with some clothes, but these were insufficient to secure me from the dews of night. I was a poor, helpless, miserable wretch; I knew, and could distinguish, nothing; but feeling pain invade me on all sides, I sat down and wept."

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how far you agree with this view: "In Frankenstein, Shelley presents the Creature as more human than the humans who reject him."

Write about:

- how Shelley presents the Creature in this extract

- how far Shelley presents the Creature as more human than those who reject him in the novel as a whole.

[30 marks]

Frame

The Creature is not born monstrous — he is born good. He is made monstrous by the people who reject him, beginning with Victor in Chapter 5. The novel's three-frame structure trains the reader to hear the Creature's eloquence as the proof Victor refuses to hear.

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• First-person narration = the speaker telling his own story ("I").

• Triadic listing / rule of three = three items in a row for emphasis ("I saw, felt, heard, and smelt" — a list of senses).

• Personification = giving non-human things human qualities ("a gentle light stole over the heavens" — the light steals like a person).

• Pathetic fallacy = nature reflecting human emotion (the "gentle light" matching the Creature's first pleasure).

• Biblical allusion = a reference back to the Bible ("a radiant form" echoing the Creation narrative).

• Self-applied epithet = a label the speaker puts on himself ("a poor, helpless, miserable wretch").

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Mary Shelley wrote Frankenstein between June 1816 and May 1817, aged eighteen, after the Villa Diodati ghost-story competition with Lord Byron and Percy Shelley on Lake Geneva — the same summer the eruption of Mount Tambora produced the "year without a summer" across Europe.

• Building sentence (historical context): Shelley's parents were William Godwin, whose Political Justice (1793) argued that human character is shaped by social circumstance, and Mary Wollstonecraft, whose Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792) extended Enlightenment arguments about education and moral standing to women — and the novel was written into a culture still debating whether monstrosity was innate or socially manufactured.

• Thesis: Across the Creature's first memories of abandonment in Volume II Chapter 11, the De Lacey rejection in Chapter 16, and his coffin-side lament in Chapter 24, Shelley presents the Creature as more human than the humans who reject him — and uses the three-frame structure to train the reader to recognise what Victor cannot.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning of the novel — 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: the Creature's first sensations and abandonment (extract, Volume II Chapter 11)

• Topic: In the Creature's earliest memories, Shelley presents him as a born-good infant whose first response to the world is sensory wonder.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "Soon a gentle light stole over the heavens and gave me a sensation of pleasure. I started up and beheld a radiant form rise from among the trees. I gazed with a kind of wonder." combines personification ("a gentle light stole" — the light moves quietly like a thoughtful person) with a biblical allusion ("a radiant form" echoing the Genesis Creation narrative as light first separates from darkness).

• Close analysis: "Gentle" and "stole" are the verbs of a careful adult arriving at a child's bedside, the universe itself seeming to greet the Creature with care; the indefinite article "a" before "radiant form" shows him without language for what the moon is, encountering it as pure wonder.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader hears a voice that does not yet sound monstrous — a newborn consciousness narrating with the quiet beauty of a Romantic poet rather than the inarticulate rage Victor's earlier framing has led the reader to expect.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader recognises the gap between Victor's "miserable monster" (Chapter 5) and the speaker now in front of them — the framing has been wrong, and the form of the novel has trained the reader to see it.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be using the Creature's own first-person voice to argue that monstrosity is constructed by language and rejection, not present at birth — the Creature's prose is the proof Victor's framing was a lie.

• Context (AO3): Godwin's Political Justice (1793) had argued that human character is shaped by social circumstance — and the Creature's articulate, sensitive narration is the Godwinian thesis spoken inside Shelley's fiction.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle of the novel — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words; the printed extract usually sits here)

Scene focus: the De Lacey rejection (Volume II Chapter 16)

• Topic: At the novel's central moral pivot, Shelley shows the Creature's humanity through his own most articulate self-diagnosis — and the institutions that refuse it.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "I am malicious because I am miserable" combines antithesis ("malicious" against "miserable" — what he becomes set against what he feels) with the causal conjunction "because" (a rational, philosophical claim that locates the source of his violence outside himself).

• Close analysis: "Malicious" names the verdict society delivers on him; "miserable" names the experience society has produced; the conjunction "because" compresses William Godwin's argument that character is shaped by circumstance into seven words — the Creature is not analysing his feelings, he is offering an argument.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader recognises that the Creature can articulate the relationship between cause and effect with a precision Victor cannot — a being supposedly without education explaining the social-circumstance thesis Victor refuses to apply to him.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader retrospectively re-reads the Chapter 5 abandonment as the moment that produced this sentence, so Victor's failure as a creator is no longer one act but a chain of refusals that runs through every door (the De Laceys, the cottage, the family Felix drives away from).

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be using the Bildungsroman shape (Volume II's compressed coming-of-age in six chapters) to argue that the institutions which produce monstrosity — family, education, custom — are the same institutions that then condemn it.

• Context (AO3): Godwin's Political Justice (1793) had argued that human character is shaped by social circumstance; Wollstonecraft's Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792) had extended that thesis to women's education; the Creature is Shelley's test case for whether her parents' radicalism could be applied to the most marginalised being she could imagine.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End of the novel — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: the Creature weeping over Victor's coffin (Volume III Chapter 24)

• Topic: At the novel's catastrophe, Shelley delivers the Creature's final humanity through his own grief over the man who refused him.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "I shall die, and what I now feel be no longer felt" combines repetition (the verb "feel" used both actively and passively in the same line) with the future-tense declaration "shall die" (the Creature naming his own ending without revenge, without rage).

• Close analysis: The shift from "feel" to "be felt" turns subject into object — the Creature ceasing to be the experiencer of suffering and becoming someone whose suffering will simply stop; the absence of any verb of self-pity in the line gives the lament a quiet dignity.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader feels the Creature's grief as the recognition Victor evades — Victor could not name what he refused (his deathbed warning misidentifies "ambition" as the wound), but the Creature names the condition society manufactured for him.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader recognises that the novel's final sympathy belongs to the antagonist and not the protagonist — the three-frame structure has done its training, and the framing Victor first delivered as "miserable monster" has been comprehensively reversed.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be using Walton's turning-back at the close of Chapter 24 to offer the moral alternative Victor refused — Walton accepts the obligation Victor declined, and the frame's closing endorses the choice the protagonist could not make.

• Context (AO3): The 1816 Villa Diodati ghost-story competition had given Shelley a competition of voices; the three-frame structure that closes the novel is the competition won by the voice nobody at Villa Diodati expected to hear.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, through the Creature's first sensations of wonder, his "I am malicious because I am miserable" self-diagnosis, and his coffin-side lament for the creator who refused him, Shelley presents the Creature as more human than the humans who reject him.

• Controlling concept: The novel's controlling argument is that creation without responsibility makes monsters, sympathy is the moral instrument human institutions refuse to provide, and the three-frame structure trains the reader to practise the moral attention its characters cannot.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be issuing a Romantic-period challenge to her readers — accept the Creature's voice and the verdict it delivers, or remain inside Victor's frame and miss the novel's argument entirely.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that monstrosity is manufactured by those who refuse to look — and the reader's task, once the framing has collapsed, is to refuse to repeat Victor's refusal.

Q2 — Victor Frankenstein as a tragic hero

Preamble

In this extract, Victor describes his obsessive pursuit of the secret of creating life.

Extract

Volume I Chapter 4 — Victor describes his obsessive pursuit of the secret of creating life:

"No one can conceive the variety of feelings which bore me onwards, like a hurricane, in the first enthusiasm of success. Life and death appeared to me ideal bounds, which I should first break through, and pour a torrent of light into our dark world. A new species would bless me as its creator and source; many happy and excellent natures would owe their being to me. No father could claim the gratitude of his child so completely as I should deserve theirs. Pursuing these reflections, I thought that if I could bestow animation upon lifeless matter, I might in process of time (although I now found it impossible) renew life where death had apparently devoted the body to corruption.

These thoughts supported my spirits, while I pursued my undertaking with unremitting ardour. My cheek had grown pale with study, and my person had become emaciated with confinement. Sometimes, on the very brink of certainty, I failed; yet still I clung to the hope which the next day or the next hour might realise. One secret which I alone possessed was the hope to which I had dedicated myself; and the moon gazed on my midnight labours, while, with unrelaxed and breathless eagerness, I pursued nature to her hiding-places. Who shall conceive the horrors of my secret toil as I dabbled among the unhallowed damps of the grave or tortured the living animal to animate the lifeless clay? My limbs now tremble, and my eyes swim with the remembrance; but then a resistless and almost frantic impulse urged me forward; I seemed to have lost all soul or sensation but for this one pursuit. It was indeed but a passing trance, that only made me feel with renewed acuteness so soon as, the unnatural stimulus ceasing to operate, I had returned to my old habits. I collected bones from charnel-houses and disturbed, with profane fingers, the tremendous secrets of the human frame. In a solitary chamber, or rather cell, at the top of the house, and separated from all the other apartments by a gallery and staircase, I kept my workshop of filthy creation; my eyeballs were starting from their sockets in attending to the details of my employment. The dissecting room and the slaughter-house furnished many of my materials; and often did my human nature turn with loathing from my occupation, whilst, still urged on by an eagerness which perpetually increased, I brought my work near to a conclusion."

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how far you agree with this view: "Shelley presents Victor Frankenstein as a tragic hero, brought down by a single fatal flaw."

Write about:

- how Shelley presents Victor in this extract

- how far Shelley presents Victor as a tragic hero in the novel as a whole.

[30 marks]

Frame

The student should partly disagree with the statement. Aristotelian hamartia is an error in action, not a fatal flaw — Victor's hamartia is the moment of abandonment in Chapter 5, not the act of creation. "Tragic hero" is therefore the wrong frame: Victor is a tragic protagonist whose hamartia is moral, not characterological.

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Simile = a direct comparison using "like" or "as" ("like a hurricane").

• Passive construction = a sentence where the subject has the action done to them rather than doing it ("bore me onwards" — Victor is acted upon).

• Promethean allusion = reference to the Greek Prometheus, who stole fire from the gods (the novel's subtitle is The Modern Prometheus).

• Antithesis = balanced opposites ("Life and death"; "ardour" against "horror").

• Hyperbole = deliberate exaggeration ("pour a torrent of light into our dark world").

• Euphemism = polite language replacing something harsher ("the being I had created" replacing "the man I made and abandoned").

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Luigi Galvani's 1791 frog experiments had demonstrated that electricity could cause dead muscle to move, and Giovanni Aldini's 1803 public reanimation of an executed murderer's corpse at Newgate, London, had made the question of restoring life a genuine debate in Mary Shelley's intellectual world.

• Building sentence (historical context): Shelley wrote Frankenstein in 1816–17 in the immediate context of the vitalism debates between William Lawrence and John Abernethy at the Royal College of Surgeons, which asked whether life was a separate spiritual principle or a property of organised matter — the precise question Victor's experiment claims to have answered.

• Thesis: Across Victor's "like a hurricane" enthusiasm in Chapter 4, his abandonment in Chapter 5, and his deathbed misdiagnosis in Chapter 24, Shelley presents Victor as a tragic protagonist whose hamartia is the moral error in action of abandonment — not a "fatal flaw" of character but a chain of refusals that begins the night his creation opens its eyes.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning of the novel — 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: the obsessive pursuit (extract, Volume I Chapter 4)

• Topic: In Victor's account of his pursuit, Shelley presents ambition as a force that has already hollowed the protagonist out before the Creature exists.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "No one can conceive the variety of feelings which bore me onwards, like a hurricane, in the first enthusiasm of success" combines simile ("like a hurricane" — uncontrollable, indiscriminate, destructive) with passive construction ("bore me onwards" — Victor swept along, not steering).

• Close analysis: The simile transforms enthusiasm into a natural disaster; the passive grammar removes Victor's agency, framing his obsession as something done to him rather than chosen by him — establishing the pattern of evading responsibility that will recur at every turn.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader recognises the self-deception in Victor's language — he frames obsession as inspiration, mania as genius.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader registers, even before the Creature is born, that the man telling the story refuses to own his choices — preparing the reader to read the abandonment in the next chapter as an extension of this evasion, not a sudden lapse.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be exposing the danger of framing unchecked ambition as inspiration — arguing that the inability to distinguish between genius and mania is itself the first step toward catastrophe.

• Context (AO3): The Romantic era celebrated the inspired genius who transcended ordinary limits; Shelley interrogates this ideal by demonstrating that the same "enthusiasm" the period valorised can destroy everything it touches.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle of the novel — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words; the printed extract usually sits here)

Scene focus: the moment of abandonment (Volume I Chapter 5)

• Topic: At the play's true hamartia, Shelley presents Victor's defining error as the abandonment that follows successful creation — not the creation itself.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "Unable to endure the aspect of the being I had created, I rushed out of the room" combines euphemism ("the being I had created" — clinically distancing Victor from the man he has just made) with the verb "rushed" (a panicked, physical flight from obligation).

• Close analysis: The noun phrase "the being I had created" refuses every more specific word — "my son", "the man", even "my Creature" — using clinical abstraction to make abandonment easier; "unable" reframes a moral choice as physical incapacity, the language of a man writing his own excuse.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader feels visceral anger at a creator who flees a newborn based solely on appearance — after months of obsessive labour to bring him into being.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader recognises that this single act of fleeing is the novel's original sin — every later death (William, Justine, Henry, Elizabeth, Alphonse, Victor himself) traces back to this Chapter 5 refusal.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be arguing that creation without care constitutes the deepest moral violation — abandonment, not ambition, is what makes Victor's tragedy.

• Context (AO3): William Godwin's philosophy of moral accountability held that individuals bear absolute responsibility for the consequences of their actions; Shelley constructs Victor as a test case for that principle and demonstrates his utter failure.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End of the novel — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: Victor's deathbed warning (Volume III Chapter 24)

• Topic: At the novel's close, Shelley shows Victor still misdiagnosing his own hamartia — the deathbed anagnorisis (the moment of recognition) is partial, not complete.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "Learn from me, if not by my precepts, at least by my example, how dangerous is the acquirement of knowledge" combines the imperative "Learn from me" (Victor positioning himself as moral teacher) with the concessive "if not… at least" structure (the speaker conceding his own words may fail in the very sentence that delivers them).

• Close analysis: The imperative claims authority Victor has not earned; the concessive admits the words may be worthless even as he offers them; the noun "acquirement" treats knowledge as a thing to be possessed rather than a process requiring moral integration — and the named danger ("knowledge") misses the actual cause ("abandonment").

• Effect on audience 1: The reader feels the irony of a man positioning himself as a teacher of others having refused to learn from his own catastrophe.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader recognises that Victor's recognition is incomplete — and that Walton, who hears it, will need to decide whether to accept Victor's wrong lesson or read against it (which Walton's turning-back ultimately suggests he does).

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be arguing that Victor's warning misidentifies the danger — the problem was never the acquirement of knowledge but the abandonment of responsibility for what that knowledge produced.

• Context (AO3): The novel's epistolary frame means this warning reaches the reader through Walton's letters, creating a transmission chain that tests whether cautionary tales can prevent their own repetition — a question the Romantic period asked of all moral literature.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, through Victor's "like a hurricane" enthusiasm, his Chapter 5 abandonment, and his Chapter 24 deathbed misdiagnosis, Shelley presents Victor as a tragic protagonist whose hamartia is the moral error in action of abandonment — not the "fatal flaw" the statement implies.

• Controlling concept: The novel's controlling argument is that creation without responsibility makes monsters — and the responsibility Victor refuses is parental, not intellectual.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be using the language of Aristotelian tragedy to argue that the Enlightenment faith in unchecked scientific progress lacks the moral framework Romantic ethics requires — knowledge without responsibility is dangerous, but the danger is the abandonment, not the knowledge.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that the man who frames obsession as inspiration, rejection as physical incapacity, and abandonment as misfortune cannot be a tragic hero in the heroic sense — only a tragic protagonist whose recognition arrives too late to redeem him.

Q3 — Creation and responsibility

Preamble

In this extract, the Creature confronts Victor on the Mer de Glace above Chamonix and demands that Victor accept his responsibility.

Extract

Volume II Chapter 10 — the Creature confronts Victor on the Mer de Glace above Chamonix:

""I expected this reception," said the dæmon. "All men hate the wretched; how, then, must I be hated, who am miserable beyond all living things! Yet you, my creator, detest and spurn me, thy creature, to whom thou art bound by ties only dissoluble by the annihilation of one of us. You purpose to kill me. How dare you sport thus with life? Do your duty towards me, and I will do mine towards you and the rest of mankind. If you will comply with my conditions, I will leave them and you at peace; but if you refuse, I will glut the maw of death, until it be satiated with the blood of your remaining friends."

Abhorred monster! Fiend that thou art! The tortures of hell are too mild a vengeance for thy crimes. Wretched devil! You reproach me with your creation, come on, then, that I may extinguish the spark which I so negligently bestowed.

My rage was without bounds; I sprang on him, impelled by all the feelings which can arm one being against the existence of another.

He easily eluded me and said,"

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how far you agree with this view: "In Frankenstein, Shelley presents Victor's greatest crime as not creating the Creature but abandoning him."

Write about:

- how Shelley presents the theme of creation and responsibility in this extract

- how far Shelley presents abandonment as Victor's greatest crime in the novel as a whole.

[30 marks]

Frame

The student should strongly agree with the statement. Aristotelian hamartia is an error in action, and Victor's specific error is the Chapter 5 abandonment. The Mer de Glace extract is the Creature's articulate demand that Victor accept the responsibility he has refused.

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Direct address = speaking to a specific person ("my creator"; "thou").

• Archaic register = old-fashioned formal language ("thou art"; "thy creature") — the Creature speaks like a Miltonic figure.

• Conditional clause = a clause beginning with "if" ("if you will comply"; "but if you refuse") — the Creature offering a choice.

• Antithesis = balanced opposites ("my creator" against "thy creature").

• Imperative = a command ("Do your duty towards me").

• Visceral imagery = imagery of the body ("glut the maw of death").

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): William Godwin's Political Justice (1793) had argued that human character is shaped by social circumstance and that institutions must therefore be reformed; Mary Wollstonecraft's Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792) had extended Enlightenment political claims to women's education and civil standing.

• Building sentence (historical context): Mary Shelley was the daughter of both, and Frankenstein (1818) was written into the period's debate about moral accountability — the philosophical question of whether the people who bring others into the world owe them anything more than existence.

• Thesis: Across Victor's two-year obsessive labour in Chapter 4, the moment of abandonment in Chapter 5, and the Mer de Glace confrontation in Chapter 10, Shelley presents Victor's greatest crime as the abandonment of his creation — not the act of creation itself, but the immediate, total refusal to accept any responsibility for what he has brought into being.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning of the novel — 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: Chapter 5 — the moment of abandonment

• Topic: In the moment of animation, Shelley shows Victor's defining failure — "I rushed out of the room" — as the original sin from which every later tragedy flows.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "Unable to endure the aspect of the being I had created, I rushed out of the room" combines euphemism ("the being I had created" — clinically distancing Victor from the man he has just made) with the verb "rushed" (a panicked physical flight from obligation).

• Close analysis: The noun phrase "the being I had created" refuses every more specific word; the participle "unable" reframes cowardice as physical incapacity; the verb "rushed" turns moral failure into a single physical motion.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader feels visceral anger at a creator who cannot endure even one moment of his creation's existence — after months of obsessive labour to bring him into being.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader recognises that this is the novel's hamartia — abandonment, not creation — because every subsequent death traces back to this single refusal in Chapter 5.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be establishing abandonment, not creation, as Victor's defining act — a violation of the Godwinian principle that creators bear absolute obligation toward what they bring into existence.

• Context (AO3): Wollstonecraft's writings on parental responsibility and Godwin's philosophy of moral accountability directly inform this moment — Victor violates the foundational principle of his mother's and father's intellectual inheritance.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle of the novel — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words; the printed extract usually sits here)

Scene focus: the Mer de Glace confrontation (Volume II Chapter 10)

• Topic: At the novel's central moral pivot, Shelley shows the Creature articulating the responsibility Victor has refused, in language that demands recognition rather than violence.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "Yet you, my creator, detest and spurn me, thy creature, to whom thou art bound by ties only dissoluble by the annihilation of one of us" combines antithesis ("my creator" set against "thy creature") with the archaic register "thou art" (Miltonic, biblical, elevating the demand to the level of the Genesis creation).

• Close analysis: The possessive "my creator" and the reciprocal "thy creature" establish a bond the Creature insists is binding; "only dissoluble by the annihilation of one of us" names the bond as absolute — only death can break it; the archaic "thou" lifts the language out of contemporary speech and into the register of Paradise Lost, the book the Creature has been reading.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader hears the Creature speak with a moral seriousness Victor has never matched — the Creature can name the bond Victor wants to deny.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader recognises the imperative "Do your duty towards me" as the precise demand Victor has been refusing since Chapter 5 — and realises that this is the chance Victor still has to repair his original failure.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be using the Creature's articulate Miltonic register (echoing Adam appealing to God in Paradise Lost) to argue that the bond between creator and creation is theological as well as moral — and that Victor, by refusing it, is refusing the foundation of moral responsibility itself.

• Context (AO3): Milton's Paradise Lost (1667) — which the Creature reads at the De Lacey cottage — provides the framework: when Adam appeals to God for justice, the appeal is granted; when the Creature appeals to Victor, the appeal is refused, completing the inversion.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End of the novel — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: the destruction of the female creature (Volume III Chapter 20)

• Topic: At the novel's later refusal, Shelley shows Victor compounding his original abandonment — destroying the female creature he had promised to make, guaranteeing the catastrophe that follows.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "As I looked on him, his countenance expressed the utmost extent of malice and treachery. I thought with a sensation of madness on my promise of creating another like to him, and trembling with passion, tore to pieces the thing on which I was engaged" combines visceral imagery ("tore to pieces" — physical violence enacted on the half-made female creature) with the abstract moral language of "malice and treachery" (the labels Victor needs to apply to justify breaking his promise).

• Close analysis: "Tore to pieces" is the verb of slaughter, not destruction — Victor undoes the body the way he might undo an enemy; "with a sensation of madness" locates the act in feeling rather than judgement; "the thing" is the second euphemism in the novel — the female creature, like the male, refused her name.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader recognises that Victor is repeating his Chapter 5 hamartia at a higher cost — the second refusal guarantees the wedding-night murder of Elizabeth and the deaths that follow.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader hears Victor's labels ("malice and treachery") as the same kind of pre-emptive verdict that produced "miserable monster" in Chapter 5 — language used to construct the monstrosity Victor refuses to recognise as his own product.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be arguing that Victor's pattern is not one isolated abandonment but a sequence — every test of his moral imagination produces the same answer.

• Context (AO3): The novel's three-frame structure means the reader meets this scene through Victor's own narration; the reader has by now learned to read against Victor's framing, recognising in "malice and treachery" the language that has always preceded Victor's refusals of responsibility.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, through the Chapter 5 abandonment, the Mer de Glace confrontation, and the destruction of the female creature in Chapter 20, Shelley presents Victor's greatest crime as the abandonment of his creation — not the act of creation itself.

• Controlling concept: The novel's controlling argument is that creation without responsibility makes monsters — and that responsibility, in the Godwinian sense, is the bond between creator and creation that only death can dissolve.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be writing into the period's debate about moral accountability — Wollstonecraft's and Godwin's argument that those who bring others into the world owe them more than existence.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that the act of creation is morally neutral — what damns Victor is the abandonment that follows.

Q4 — Monstrosity (socially constructed, not innate)

Preamble

In this extract, Victor sees his animated Creature for the first time and recoils.

Extract

Volume I Chapter 5 — Victor's first sight of the animated Creature:

"How can I describe my emotions at this catastrophe, or how delineate the wretch whom with such infinite pains and care I had endeavoured to form? His limbs were in proportion, and I had selected his features as beautiful. Beautiful! Great God! His yellow skin scarcely covered the work of muscles and arteries beneath; his hair was of a lustrous black, and flowing; his teeth of a pearly whiteness; but these luxuriances only formed a more horrid contrast with his watery eyes, that seemed almost of the same colour as the dun-white sockets in which they were set, his shrivelled complexion and straight black lips.

The different accidents of life are not so changeable as the feelings of human nature. I had worked hard for nearly two years, for the sole purpose of infusing life into an inanimate body. For this I had deprived myself of rest and health. I had desired it with an ardour that far exceeded moderation; but now that I had finished, the beauty of the dream vanished, and breathless horror and disgust filled my heart. Unable to endure the aspect of the being I had created, I rushed out of the room and continued a long time traversing my bed-chamber, unable to compose my mind to sleep. At length lassitude succeeded to the tumult I had before endured, and I threw myself on the bed in my clothes, endeavouring to seek a few moments of forgetfulness. But it was in vain; I slept, indeed, but I was disturbed by the wildest dreams. I thought I saw Elizabeth, in the bloom of health, walking in the streets of Ingolstadt. Delighted and surprised, I embraced her, but as I imprinted the first kiss on her lips, they became livid with the hue of death; her features appeared to change, and I thought that I held the corpse of my dead mother in my arms; a shroud enveloped her form, and I saw the grave-worms crawling in the folds of the flannel. I started from my sleep with horror; a cold dew covered my forehead, my teeth chattered, and every limb became convulsed; when, by the dim and yellow light of the moon, as it forced its way through the window shutters, I beheld the wretch—the miserable monster whom I had created. He held up the curtain of the bed; and his eyes, if eyes they may be called, were fixed on me. His jaws opened, and he muttered some inarticulate sounds, while a grin wrinkled his cheeks. He might have spoken, but I did not hear; one hand was stretched out, seemingly to detain me, but I escaped and rushed downstairs. I took refuge in the courtyard belonging to the house which I inhabited, where I remained during the rest of the night, walking up and down in the greatest agitation, listening attentively, catching and fearing each sound as if it were to announce the approach of the demoniacal corpse to which I had so miserably given life."

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Shelley presents the theme of monstrosity in Frankenstein.

Write about:

- how Shelley presents monstrosity in this extract

- how Shelley presents monstrosity in the novel as a whole.

[30 marks]

Frame

Monstrosity is socially constructed, not innate. Victor names the Creature a monster before he has performed a single action — and the language of that naming ("miserable monster", "wretch") is what manufactures the monstrosity it claims to perceive.

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Alliteration = repetition of the same starting sound ("miserable monster" — both words begin with "m").

• Pejorative noun = an insulting noun used as a label ("wretch").

• Antithesis = balanced opposites ("beautiful" against "horrid"; "luxuriances" against "watery eyes").

• Pre-emptive labelling = applying a label before the labelled person has acted to deserve it.

• Aesthetic register = language about beauty / ugliness, not about morals ("yellow skin", "shrivelled complexion").

• Foreshadowing = early hints of what is to come.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): William Godwin's Political Justice (1793) had argued that human character is shaped by social circumstance — the philosophical position that came to be called circumstantial determinism.

• Building sentence (historical context): Frankenstein was written in 1816–17 in the immediate aftermath of the Industrial Revolution's reduction of complex wholes to mechanical components, and into a literary culture that increasingly debated whether what looks monstrous is monstrous — or whether monstrosity is the verdict society delivers before any monstrous act has been committed.

• Thesis: Across Victor's "miserable monster" labelling in Chapter 5, the De Lacey rejection in Chapter 16, and the Creature's coffin-side lament in Chapter 24, Shelley presents monstrosity as socially constructed — Victor names the Creature a monster before any action justifies the label, and the rest of the novel dramatises the consequences of that naming.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning of the novel — 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: Victor's first labelling (extract, Volume I Chapter 5)

• Topic: In the animation scene, Shelley presents monstrosity as the work of language and aesthetic judgement, not the work of the Creature.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "His limbs were in proportion, and I had selected his features as beautiful. Beautiful! — Great God!" combines antithesis ("beautiful" against the implicit "horrid" the exclamation triggers) with repetition ("Beautiful! — Great God!" — the word repeated as exclamation, the same noun used to mean its opposite).

• Close analysis: The past tense "I had selected his features as beautiful" establishes that the features are objectively beautiful; the exclamation "Beautiful! — Great God!" converts the same word into a horrified rebuke; the Creature's body has not changed, only Victor's perception of it — exposing that the monstrosity resides in the viewer, not the viewed.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader recognises the gap between intended beauty and perceived horror as a failure of Victor's imagination, not a failure of the Creature's body.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader registers the alliteration of "miserable monster" (Chapter 5, slightly later) as a rhythmic condemnation — the m-sounds binding the words into a single label that will travel through the rest of the novel.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be arguing that monstrosity is first manufactured through language — Victor names the Creature a monster before any monstrous act occurs, exposing how labelling precedes and produces exclusion.

• Context (AO3): The Romantic era debated whether language shapes perception or merely reflects it; Shelley dramatises the former, aligning with Rousseau's argument that social constructs determine how beings are treated.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle of the novel — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words; the printed extract usually sits here)

Scene focus: the De Lacey rejection (Volume II Chapter 16)

• Topic: At the novel's central social rejection, Shelley shows the Creature's self-application of the labels society has imposed — monstrosity becoming the verdict the excluded internalise.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "I, the miserable and the abandoned, am an abortion" combines self-applied epithets ("the miserable and the abandoned" — definite articles universalising private pain) with metaphor ("an abortion" — a life that should never have existed).

• Close analysis: The definite articles "the miserable" and "the abandoned" turn the Creature into a category rather than a person; "abortion" is the harshest possible self-noun, a metaphor of incompleteness inherited from the verdict society delivers.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader feels devastated that the Creature has absorbed society's verdict so completely that he defines himself through their rejection.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader recognises the Godwinian thesis in fictional form — that sustained exclusion forces the excluded to internalise the labels imposed upon them, transforming external prejudice into self-destruction.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be demonstrating the precise mechanism by which monstrosity becomes monstrosity — through repeated rejection, the labels the Creature has heard from Victor, the De Laceys and the villagers become the labels he applies to himself.

• Context (AO3): The early nineteenth century debated whether the poor and marginalised were innately inferior or socially damaged; the Creature embodies the argument that social conditions manufacture identity, the central claim of Political Justice (1793).

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End of the novel — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: the coffin-side lament (Volume III Chapter 24)

• Topic: At the novel's close, Shelley delivers the final inversion — the "monster" mourns the man who refused him, and the framing Victor first applied collapses.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "I shall die, and what I now feel be no longer felt. Soon these burning miseries will be extinct" combines the future-tense declaration "I shall die" (the Creature naming his own ending) with metaphor ("burning miseries" — feelings imagined as fire, capable of being "extinct").

• Close analysis: "What I now feel be no longer felt" converts subject into object — the Creature ceasing to be the experiencer of suffering; "burning miseries" gives feelings physicality; "extinct" is the verb of fire ending, of light ceasing — a Romantic image of grief as combustion.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader hears the Creature speak with a moral seriousness and poetic register that the "miserable monster" label of Chapter 5 had refused to acknowledge could exist in him.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader recognises that the framing has been comprehensively reversed — Victor could not name what he refused, but the Creature names his own condition with the precision of a Romantic poet.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be arguing that the "monster" of the opening is the most fully human voice in the novel's closing — and that the framing the reader has been trained to read against finally collapses entirely.

• Context (AO3): Wollstonecraft's arguments about the damage of judging women by appearance rather than character extend, in Shelley's hands, to all marginalised beings — a society that equates beauty with worth will inevitably produce the violence it fears.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, through Victor's first labelling in Chapter 5, the Creature's self-applied "abortion" in Chapter 16, and his coffin-side lament in Chapter 24, Shelley presents monstrosity as socially constructed — the verdict society delivers before any monstrous act has been committed, then internalised by the rejected.

• Controlling concept: The novel's controlling argument is that creation without responsibility makes monsters — and the making happens through language and rejection, not through the body of the created.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be writing into the period's debate about whether character is innate or shaped, arguing that monstrosity is manufactured by those who label, not present in those they label.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that a society that equates beauty with worth, and ugliness with sin, will inevitably produce the violence it fears.

Q5 — Isolation

Preamble

In this extract, Walton writes to his sister Margaret Saville from the edge of the Arctic and admits the want of a friend.

Extract

Walton's first letter to his sister Margaret Saville (Letter 2):

"But I have one want which I have never yet been able to satisfy, and the absence of the object of which I now feel as a most severe evil, I have no friend, Margaret: when I am glowing with the enthusiasm of success, there will be none to participate my joy; if I am assailed by disappointment, no one will endeavour to sustain me in dejection. I shall commit my thoughts to paper, it is true; but that is a poor medium for the communication of feeling. I desire the company of a man who could sympathise with me, whose eyes would reply to mine. You may deem me romantic, my dear sister, but I bitterly feel the want of a friend. I have no one near me, gentle yet courageous, possessed of a cultivated as well as of a capacious mind, whose tastes are like my own, to approve or amend my plans. How would such a friend repair the faults of your poor brother! I am too ardent in execution and too impatient of difficulties. But it is a still greater evil to me that I am self-educated: for the first fourteen years of my life I ran wild on a common and read nothing but our Uncle Thomas' books of voyages. At that age I became acquainted with the celebrated poets of our own country; but it was only when it had ceased to be in my power to derive its most important benefits from such a conviction that I perceived the necessity of becoming acquainted with more languages than that of my native country. Now I am twenty-eight and am in reality more illiterate than many schoolboys of fifteen. It is true that I have thought more and that my daydreams are more extended and magnificent, but they want (as the painters call it) _keeping;_ and I greatly need a friend who would have sense enough not to despise me as romantic, and affection enough for me to endeavour to regulate my mind.

Well, these are useless complaints; I shall certainly find no friend on the wide ocean, nor even here in Archangel, among merchants and seamen. Yet some feelings, unallied to the dross of human nature, beat even in these rugged bosoms. My lieutenant, for instance, is a man of wonderful courage and enterprise; he is madly desirous of glory, or rather, to word my phrase more characteristically, of advancement in his profession. He is an Englishman, and in the midst of national and professional prejudices, unsoftened by cultivation, retains some of the noblest endowments of humanity. I first became acquainted with him on board a whale vessel; finding that he was unemployed in this city, I easily engaged him to assist in my enterprise."

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Shelley presents the theme of isolation in Frankenstein.

Write about:

- how Shelley presents isolation in this extract

- how Shelley presents isolation in the novel as a whole.

[30 marks]

Frame

Isolation is the foundational condition of every tragedy in the novel — Walton's, Victor's, and the Creature's. The novel opens in isolation, and every destructive choice is made by a character without the human relationship that could have moderated it.

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Epistolary form = a novel told through letters (Walton's letters frame the whole story).

• Confessional register = the speaker telling intimate truths to a known reader (here, Walton's sister).

• Direct address = speaking to a named person ("Margaret").

• Declarative simplicity = short, plain sentences that strip away pretence ("I have no friend, Margaret").

• Foreshadowing = early hints of what is to come (Walton's Arctic isolation prefiguring Victor's).

• Triadic structure / rule of three = three items in a sequence ("gentle yet courageous, possessed of a cultivated as well as of a capacious mind, whose tastes are like my own").

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): The Romantic era — into which Mary Shelley wrote Frankenstein in 1816–17 — celebrated the inspired solitary genius: Wordsworth's I wandered lonely as a cloud (1807), Byron's wandering exile, Coleridge's solitary mariner, all elevated isolation to a creative ideal.

• Building sentence (historical context): Yet the same period also feared the consequences of solitary ambition — the unchecked discoverer, the figure whose pursuit of knowledge or power was unmoderated by human relationship — and Frankenstein dramatises this Romantic tension, opening with a polar expedition designed to test how far the solitary genius can go before the cost becomes unsurvivable.

• Thesis: Across Walton's "I have no friend, Margaret" in Letter 2, Victor's "I seemed to have lost all soul or sensation but for this one pursuit" in Chapter 4, and the Creature's coffin-side lament in Chapter 24, Shelley presents isolation as the foundational condition that precedes and enables every destructive choice in the novel.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning of the novel — 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: Walton's letter (extract, Letter 2)

• Topic: In the novel's opening letters, Shelley establishes isolation as the condition that frames every other character's tragedy.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "I have no friend, Margaret" combines declarative simplicity (a five-word sentence stripped of qualification) with direct address ("Margaret" — naming the person Walton is writing to, the sister he cannot reach).

• Close analysis: The bare declaration removes every comfort of complexity — there is nothing to soften the truth; the address to Margaret by name reminds the reader that the letter is reaching her only because Walton lacks a present companion to speak the same words to in person.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader recognises immediately that the novel opens in solitude — Walton's Arctic expedition replicates the isolation that will define both Victor's creation and the Creature's existence.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader recognises the letter form itself as the proof of the isolation it describes — letters exist because the person speaking is not in the room with the person listening.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be using the epistolary frame to establish from the opening sentence that the absence of human connection is the precondition for every destructive choice in the novel.

• Context (AO3): The Romantic era celebrated individual genius but feared its consequences; Walton embodies the tension between the drive for solitary discovery and the human need for companionship.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle of the novel — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words; the printed extract usually sits here)

Scene focus: Victor's chosen isolation (Volume I Chapter 4)

• Topic: At the novel's central self-imposed exile, Shelley shows Victor's isolation as a deliberate choice, not a circumstance — making his solitude more culpable than the Creature's enforced exile.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "I seemed to have lost all soul or sensation but for this one pursuit" combines abstract nouns ("soul" and "sensation" encompassing spiritual identity and physical awareness) with the hedge "seemed" (epistemic doubt that functions as Victor's self-exculpation).

• Close analysis: "Lost" implies passive victimhood — Victor framing himself as something done to; "seemed" introduces uncertainty about whether the loss was real, allowing Victor to claim the loss as both fact and excuse; "but for this one pursuit" narrows existence to a single obsessive channel that excluded family and friends.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader feels unease at Victor's willingness to surrender his humanity for knowledge — recognising he had hollowed himself out before the Creature even existed.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader recognises that Victor's earlier "No human being could have passed a happier childhood than myself" (Chapter 1) makes his Chapter 4 isolation self-inflicted — he was not denied connection, he chose to abandon it.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be critiquing the Enlightenment faith in unchecked scientific progress by demonstrating that knowledge pursued without ethical relationship does not elevate humanity but erodes it.

• Context (AO3): The 1816 conversations about Galvanism at the Villa Diodati raised genuine questions about whether scientists could reanimate the dead; Shelley responded not with wonder but with warning about the moral vacancy of those who pursue such power alone.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End of the novel — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: the Creature's enforced isolation (Volume II Chapter 16 + Volume III Chapter 24)

• Topic: At the novel's close, Shelley delivers the third register of isolation — the Creature's enforced exile, the loneliness produced by every door that closes against him.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "I am alone and miserable; man will not associate with me" combines parallel clauses ("I am alone" and "man will not associate with me" — two ways of stating the same condition) with the universal noun "man" (every human being, not a specific individual).

• Close analysis: The simple "alone" and "miserable" are the same kind of bare adjectives Walton uses in his letter — the Creature speaks the same vocabulary of loneliness Walton speaks in Letter 2, and Victor speaks in Chapter 4; "man will not associate with me" generalises the rejection into a universal — every door, not one specific door.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader recognises the Creature's isolation as the direct continuation of Walton's and Victor's — three characters in the same novel using the same lexicon to describe the same condition.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader recognises that the Creature's isolation is the only one in the novel that is not chosen — and that this unchosen isolation makes his subsequent violence the most morally complex of all the novel's tragedies.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be arguing that the structural shape of the novel — three frames, three voices, three isolations — is itself the argument: every catastrophe in the novel happens to a character speaking alone.

• Context (AO3): Rousseau's philosophy of childhood innocence and connection had framed the early years as humanity's most connected state; the Creature is denied even that — born into solitude with no parent, no community, no language.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, through Walton's letter, Victor's chosen self-exile, and the Creature's enforced solitude, Shelley presents isolation as the foundational condition that precedes and enables every destructive choice in the novel.

• Controlling concept: The novel's controlling argument is that creation without responsibility makes monsters, sympathy is the moral instrument human institutions refuse to provide — and isolation is the precise condition under which both refusals occur.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be using the three-frame structure to argue that the form of the novel is itself the lesson: stories told in isolation produce the catastrophes only relationship could have prevented.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that ambition unchecked by human connection — Walton's Arctic, Victor's laboratory, the Creature's exile — produces the same kind of damage in different bodies.

Q6 — The pursuit of knowledge

Preamble

In this extract, Walton opens his first letter to his sister Margaret Saville on setting out for the Arctic.

Extract

Walton's first letter (Letter 1):

"I cannot describe to you my sensations on the near prospect of my undertaking. It is impossible to communicate to you a conception of the trembling sensation, half pleasurable and half fearful, with which I am preparing to depart. I am going to unexplored regions, to "the land of mist and snow," but I shall kill no albatross; therefore do not be alarmed for my safety or if I should come back to you as worn and woeful as the "Ancient Mariner." You will smile at my allusion, but I will disclose a secret. I have often attributed my attachment to, my passionate enthusiasm for, the dangerous mysteries of ocean to that production of the most imaginative of modern poets. There is something at work in my soul which I do not understand. I am practically industrious—painstaking, a workman to execute with perseverance and labour—but besides this there is a love for the marvellous, a belief in the marvellous, intertwined in all my projects, which hurries me out of the common pathways of men, even to the wild sea and unvisited regions I am about to explore.

But to return to dearer considerations. Shall I meet you again, after having traversed immense seas, and returned by the most southern cape of Africa or America? I dare not expect such success, yet I cannot bear to look on the reverse of the picture. Continue for the present to write to me by every opportunity: I may receive your letters on some occasions when I need them most to support my spirits. I love you very tenderly. Remember me with affection, should you never hear from me again."

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Shelley presents the pursuit of knowledge in Frankenstein.

Write about:

- how Shelley presents the pursuit of knowledge in this extract

- how Shelley presents the pursuit of knowledge in the novel as a whole.

[30 marks]

Frame

Knowledge in Frankenstein is morally neutral — what makes it dangerous is the absence of relational and ethical restraint. Walton, Victor and the Creature each pursue knowledge, and the novel measures their pursuits not by the knowledge sought but by the relationships sustained alongside it.

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Abstract noun = a word for something you cannot touch ("soul", "sensation", "glory", "knowledge").

• Personification = giving non-human things human qualities ("something at work in my soul" — "something" is treated like a worker).

• Tricolon / rule of three = three items in a row ("painstaking, a workman to execute with perseverance and labour").

• Imperative = a command ("Learn from me").

• Concessive clause = a clause beginning with "if not… at least" (admitting weakness even as the speaker claims authority).

• Promethean allusion = reference to Prometheus, who stole fire from the gods (the novel's subtitle is The Modern Prometheus).

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): The 1816 vitalism debates between William Lawrence and John Abernethy at the Royal College of Surgeons asked whether life was a separate spiritual principle or a property of organised matter — a public scientific controversy that ran in the years Mary Shelley was composing Frankenstein.

• Building sentence (historical context): The Enlightenment had celebrated scientific progress as inherently beneficial — Bacon's advancement of learning, Newton's mathematical universe — but the Romantic era inherited that confidence alongside an emerging anxiety about its costs, and Shelley wrote Frankenstein into that exact tension.

• Thesis: Across Walton's "something at work in my soul" in Letter 1, Victor's "a torrent of light into our dark world" in Chapter 4, and Victor's "Learn from me" deathbed warning in Chapter 24, Shelley presents the pursuit of knowledge as morally neutral — and dangerous only when it operates without relational or ethical restraint.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning of the novel — 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: Walton's letter (extract, Letter 1)

• Topic: In the opening letter, Shelley presents the pursuit of knowledge as an unconscious compulsion the seeker cannot fully name.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "There is something at work in my soul which I do not understand" combines personification ("something at work" — an impersonal force treated like a worker labouring inside Walton) with the abstract noun "soul" (elevating intellectual restlessness to a spiritual condition).

• Close analysis: "Something" refuses to name the force driving Walton — knowledge unspecified, desire unspecified; "at work" metaphorises that force as labour Walton himself is not performing; the negation "I do not understand" concedes the speaker has no insight into his own motive.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader feels foreboding — a man driven by impulses he cannot name toward knowledge he cannot yet define is a man who cannot predict the consequences of his pursuit.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader hears Walton's "the love for the marvellous" (a few lines later) as Romantic-period code for Promethean ambition — the desire to transcend ordinary limits, the desire that destroyed Victor.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be arguing from the novel's opening that the pursuit of knowledge begins as an unconscious compulsion rather than a rational choice, warning that desire unchecked by self-awareness is inherently dangerous.

• Context (AO3): The Romantic preoccupation with the sublime and the unconscious mind treated the unknowable interior as both genius and threat; Shelley dramatises the danger of pursuing external knowledge without first understanding the internal forces driving the pursuit.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle of the novel — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words; the printed extract usually sits here)

Scene focus: Victor's torrent of light (Volume I Chapter 4)

• Topic: At the novel's central pursuit, Shelley shows Victor framing his ambition as humanitarian glory while the language reveals it as the desire for personal recognition.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "Wealth was an inferior object, but what glory would attend the discovery if I could banish disease from the human frame" combines false modesty (the dismissal of "wealth" as "inferior", positioning Victor above material gain) with the abstract noun "glory" (the actual motivation, betrayed by the word's conspicuous priority over the apparent humanitarian goal).

• Close analysis: "Inferior object" is a concessive gesture — Victor concedes wealth in order to claim something higher; the conditional "if I could" frames godlike power as aspiration; "banish disease from the human frame" sounds humanitarian, but the noun "glory" gives the game away — the intended audience is not humanity, it is Victor's reputation in the eyes of humanity.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader detects self-deception — Victor frames the pursuit of knowledge as service to humanity while the language reveals it as a pursuit of fame.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader recognises the Promethean allusion — Victor is The Modern Prometheus of the subtitle, the figure who steals fire from the gods to bring glory upon himself, regardless of the cost to those around him.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be exposing how the rhetoric of beneficial knowledge conceals individual ego — arguing that the desire to know is rarely as altruistic as it claims.

• Context (AO3): The Enlightenment celebrated scientific progress as inherently beneficial; Shelley questions whether the desire to conquer death is truly altruistic or merely Promethean hubris disguised as philanthropy.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End of the novel — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: Victor's deathbed warning (Volume III Chapter 24)

• Topic: At the novel's close, Shelley shows Victor misidentifying the danger of his pursuit — naming knowledge as the wound when the wound was abandonment.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "Learn from me, if not by my precepts, at least by my example, how dangerous is the acquirement of knowledge" combines the imperative "Learn from me" (Victor positioning himself as moral teacher) with the concessive clause "if not by my precepts, at least by my example" (the speaker conceding his own words may fail in the very sentence that delivers them).

• Close analysis: The imperative claims authority Victor has not earned; the concessive admits the words may be worthless even as he offers them; the noun "acquirement" treats knowledge as a thing to be possessed rather than a process requiring moral integration — and the named danger is the wrong danger, since the disaster came from abandonment, not from learning.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader feels the irony of a man positioning himself as a teacher of others having refused to learn from his own catastrophe.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader registers the warning as unreliable — and Walton's later turning-back of his ship, which reverses Victor's path rather than repeating it, suggests that Walton has heard the warning correctly even though Victor delivered it incorrectly.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be arguing that the danger lies not in the acquirement of knowledge but in the abandonment of responsibility for what knowledge produces — and that Victor's warning, by misnaming the danger, would condemn the next Walton to repeat the same mistake.

• Context (AO3): The novel's epistolary frame means this warning reaches the reader through Walton, creating a transmission chain that tests whether cautionary tales can prevent their own repetition — a question the Romantic period asked of all moral literature.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, through Walton's "something at work in my soul", Victor's "glory would attend the discovery", and Victor's deathbed "Learn from me", Shelley presents the pursuit of knowledge as morally neutral and dangerous only when it operates without relational or ethical restraint.

• Controlling concept: The novel's controlling argument is that creation without responsibility makes monsters — and the responsibility refused is the relational restraint that knowledge alone cannot supply.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be writing into the Romantic-period tension between the celebration of solitary genius and the fear of unchecked ambition — using Walton's ultimate turning-back as the moral alternative Victor refused.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that the danger of the pursuit of knowledge is not the knowledge but the absence of the relationships that should restrain it.
