Question

Starting with this extract, explore how far you agree with this view: "In Frankenstein, Shelley demonstrates that environment and circumstance, not innate character, determine who a being becomes."

Write about:

- how Shelley presents the theme of nature versus nurture in this extract

- how far Shelley demonstrates this view in the novel as a whole.

[30 marks]

Frame

The student should agree with the statement. The Creature is born good in Volume II's reading-list Bildungsroman, and made malicious by repeated rejection. The novel is the most direct dramatisation of William Godwin's circumstantial-determinism thesis in early-nineteenth-century English fiction.

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Antithesis = balanced opposites ("malicious" against "miserable").

• Causal conjunction = a connecting word that names the cause-and-effect ("because").

• Rhetorical question = a question used to argue rather than to ask ("Am I not shunned and hated by all mankind?").

• Apostrophe = direct address to someone absent ("Cursed creator!").

• Triadic listing = three items in a row.

• Bildungsroman = a novel of moral and intellectual formation; the form Volume II deploys for the Creature's compressed coming-of-age.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): William Godwin's An Enquiry Concerning Political Justice (1793) had argued that human character is shaped by social circumstance rather than innate disposition — a position that came to be called circumstantial determinism and that informed every page of his daughter Mary Shelley's novel.

• Building sentence (historical context): Mary Wollstonecraft's A Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792) had extended that thesis specifically to women's education — arguing that women appeared morally and intellectually weaker only because they had been taught to be — and Frankenstein (1818) tests both arguments simultaneously by inventing a being denied every form of nurture and tracing what he becomes.

• Thesis: Across the Creature's "I am malicious because I am miserable" in Volume II Chapter 16, his reading-list Bildungsroman in Chapters 11–15, and his coffin-side mourning for Victor in Chapter 24, Shelley demonstrates that environment and circumstance — not innate character — determine who a being becomes.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning of the novel — 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: the Creature's first awareness (Volume II Chapter 11)

• Topic: In the Creature's earliest memories, Shelley shows that his initial nature is good — sensory wonder, gratitude for warmth, immediate sympathy with the De Lacey family.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "Soon a gentle light stole over the heavens and gave me a sensation of pleasure" combines personification ("a gentle light stole" — the moonlight personified as a careful adult presence) with the abstract noun "pleasure" (the Creature's first response to the world is positive emotion, not negative).

• Close analysis: "Gentle" and "stole" are the verbs of someone arriving carefully to a child, the universe greeting the Creature with kindness; "sensation of pleasure" registers the response in his body — pleasure precedes pain, gratitude precedes resentment.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader hears a being whose first instinct is to be moved by beauty — the opposite of the "miserable monster" Victor named in Chapter 5.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader registers the gap between the Creature's initial nature (sensory openness, capacity for wonder) and the verdict society will deliver on him (ugliness, malice) — and identifies the gap as the space where nurture was supposed to operate but did not.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be establishing the Creature as the clean test case for the Godwinian thesis — a being with no inherited family, no inherited education, no inherited culture, observed from the moment of his first sensation.

• Context (AO3): Rousseau's philosophy of childhood innocence — humanity's most natural state being its most virtuous — directly informs the Creature's opening goodness; Shelley is testing what Rousseau argued in the most extreme possible conditions.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle of the novel — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words; the printed extract usually sits here)

Scene focus: the De Lacey rejection (extract, Volume II Chapter 16)

• Topic: At the novel's central social rejection, Shelley shows the precise mechanism by which circumstance produces malice — a born-good being made monstrous by sustained rejection.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "I am malicious because I am miserable" combines antithesis ("malicious" against "miserable" — what he becomes set against what he feels) with the causal conjunction "because" (a rational, philosophical claim that locates the source of his violence outside himself).

• Close analysis: "Malicious" names the verdict society delivers; "miserable" names the experience society has produced; the conjunction "because" compresses Godwin's argument that character is shaped by circumstance into seven words — the Creature is not analysing his feelings, he is offering an argument.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader recognises that the Creature can articulate the relationship between cause and effect with a precision Victor cannot — a being supposedly without education explaining the social-circumstance thesis Victor refuses to apply to him.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader retrospectively re-reads the Chapter 5 abandonment as the moment that produced this sentence, and the De Lacey rejection as the moment that confirmed it — circumstance doing its precise work across two scenes.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be using the Bildungsroman shape (Volume II's compressed coming-of-age in six chapters) to argue that the institutions which produce monstrosity — family, education, custom — are the same institutions that then condemn it.

• Context (AO3): Godwin's Political Justice (1793) argued that human character is shaped by social circumstance; the Creature's claim is the Godwinian thesis spoken in fiction by the being most marginalised the period could imagine.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End of the novel — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: the coffin-side mourning (Volume III Chapter 24)

• Topic: At the novel's close, Shelley delivers the final inversion — the "malicious" Creature mourning the man who refused him, his nature reasserted now that the rejecting circumstance is gone.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "Once my fancy was soothed with dreams of virtue, of fame, and of enjoyment. Once I falsely hoped to meet with beings who, pardoning my outward form, would love me for the excellent qualities which I was capable of unfolding" combines triadic listing ("of virtue, of fame, and of enjoyment" — three abstract goods the Creature once hoped for) with the conditional clause "who, pardoning my outward form, would love me for the excellent qualities…" (the bargain society refused).

• Close analysis: "Once… Once…" repeated as anaphora at the start of two clauses marks the loss of two earlier hopes; "the excellent qualities which I was capable of unfolding" is the clearest possible statement of the Godwinian thesis from the Creature's own mouth — qualities present, capable of unfolding given the right circumstance, but never given that circumstance.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader hears the Creature claim he was born good and made bad — and recognises that the entire novel has been the dramatised proof of that claim.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader watches the Creature's mourning over Victor's coffin as the final evidence — the rejected being, now finally beyond the rejection, returning to grief rather than to violence.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be arguing that the Creature's nature, when finally unobstructed by sustained social rejection, returns to the virtue and enjoyment he was always capable of unfolding — proving that the malice was always circumstantial.

• Context (AO3): Wollstonecraft's argument that women's apparent inferiority was the product of denied education extends, in Shelley's novel, to all marginalised beings — the Creature is the most extreme test of his mother's thesis, and the test confirms it.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, through the Creature's first sensation of pleasure, his "I am malicious because I am miserable", and his coffin-side mourning, Shelley demonstrates that environment and circumstance — not innate character — determine who a being becomes.

• Controlling concept: The novel's controlling argument is that creation without responsibility makes monsters — and the making is done by the rejecting world, not by the rejected creature.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be writing the most direct dramatisation of circumstantial determinism in early-nineteenth-century English fiction — the Godwinian and Wollstonecraftian inheritance taken to its furthest extreme.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that the "miserable monster" of the opening is the nurture the novel's society produces; the capable of unfolding excellent qualities of the close is the nature the same society refuses to allow.

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Shelley presents the theme of revenge in Frankenstein.

Write about:

- how Shelley presents revenge in this extract

- how Shelley presents revenge in the novel as a whole.

[30 marks]

Frame

Revenge in Frankenstein is symmetrical — Victor's pursuit of the Creature mirrors the Creature's pursuit of Victor, and neither can stop. The novel argues that revenge is the form rejection takes when the rejected can no longer access any other relationship.

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Hyperbole = deliberate exaggeration ("everlasting war").

• Simile = direct comparison using "like" ("like the arch-fiend").

• Allusion = a reference back to another text ("the arch-fiend" echoes Milton's Satan in Paradise Lost).

• Triadic listing = three items in a row ("tear up the trees, spread havoc and destruction around me, and then… enjoyed the ruin").

• Antithesis = balanced opposites.

• Personification = giving non-human things human qualities ("a hell within me" — hell treated as something he carries).

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): John Milton's Paradise Lost (1667) — which the Creature reads at the De Lacey cottage in Volume II — opens with Satan's vow of "immortal hate" against God after his expulsion from heaven, an Early Modern model for revenge as the response to refused belonging.

• Building sentence (historical context): Frankenstein (1818) was written into a Romantic culture that re-read Milton's Satan as a sympathetic figure — Blake, Byron and the Shelleys all argued that Milton was "of the Devil's party without knowing it" — and Shelley positions the Creature as a sympathetic Satan, an outsider who turns to revenge because relationship has been refused him.

• Thesis: Across the Creature's "everlasting war" vow in Volume II Chapter 16, Elizabeth's wedding-night murder in Chapter 23, and the symmetrical Arctic pursuit of Chapter 24, Shelley presents revenge as the form rejection takes when the rejected can no longer access any other relationship — a force that consumes both pursuer and pursued.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning of the novel — 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: Justine's hanging (Volume I Chapter 8) — the first revenge by the Creature

• Topic: In the first execution of the Creature's revenge, Shelley shows the violence as targeted at innocents — the people Victor loves, not Victor himself.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "I gazed on my victim, and my heart swelled with exultation and hellish triumph" (the Creature, recalling William's murder) combines personification ("my heart swelled" — the heart treated as something that physically expands with feeling) with abstract nouns ("exultation" and "hellish triumph" — emotional states elevated and demonised).

• Close analysis: "Swelled" makes feeling physical, the body responding before reason can speak; "hellish" as adjective explicitly aligns the Creature with Milton's Satan, the "hell within me" of the later vow already operating; "exultation" names the brief satisfaction revenge offers — fleeting, not redemptive.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader feels horror at the targeting of William, a child — and yet, having been trained by the three-frame structure, the reader cannot dismiss the Creature's exultation as pure malice without remembering its source.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader recognises that Justine's false confession and hanging fall on the family servant, not on the Creature, not on Victor — making the institutional cost of Victor's silence the third innocent death set in motion by the Chapter 5 abandonment.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be staging revenge as circuit: a wrong is done at the top of the chain (Victor's abandonment), a violence is committed at the middle (the Creature's act), and the innocent at the bottom of the chain (Justine) carries the cost.

• Context (AO3): Godwin's Political Justice had argued that institutions which produce injustice cannot be repaired by violence aimed elsewhere — Justine's hanging by the Genevan court is the institutional confirmation that revenge spreads its damage downward.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle of the novel — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words; the printed extract usually sits here)

Scene focus: the "everlasting war" vow (extract, Volume II Chapter 16)

• Topic: At the novel's central revenge declaration, Shelley shows the Creature naming his turn from sympathy-seeker to avenger — and aligning himself with the Miltonic Satan whose role he has been reading.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "I, like the arch-fiend, bore a hell within me" combines simile ("like the arch-fiend" — the Creature directly comparing himself to Milton's Satan) with personification ("a hell within me" — hell carried inside the body, an internalised condition).

• Close analysis: "Like the arch-fiend" deliberately invokes the Paradise Lost lineage the Creature has just been reading — he is not just becoming malicious, he is modelling himself on the literary figure he most resembles; "bore" (carried) makes hell something portable, brought wherever he goes; "within me" is the precise location — internalised, not external.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader recognises the Creature has adopted Milton's framework — having read Paradise Lost at the De Lacey cottage, he can no longer encounter himself outside that mythology.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader feels the symmetry: the Creature has been called monster and now becomes monster; he has been refused relationship and now vows war; the labels have produced what they accused.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be using the Miltonic allusion to argue that revenge is the literary frame the rejected reach for when no other relationship is available — the Creature does not choose Satan, the world has forced the Satan-shape upon him.

• Context (AO3): Romantic readings of Milton — by Blake, Byron, and the Shelleys themselves — celebrated Satan as the sympathetic outsider who refused unjust authority; Shelley's Creature is the most direct Romantic-Satanic figure in the period's fiction.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End of the novel — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: Elizabeth's wedding-night murder + the Arctic pursuit (Chapters 23–24)

• Topic: At the novel's catastrophe, Shelley shows revenge consuming both pursuer and pursued — Victor and the Creature locked in a chase neither can win.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "I have devoted my creator, the select specimen of all that is worthy of love and admiration among men, to misery; I have pursued him even to that irremediable ruin" (the Creature, Chapter 24) combines apostrophe ("my creator" — direct address to the absent Victor) with the totalising adjective phrase "the select specimen of all that is worthy of love and admiration among men" (the Creature naming Victor as the very humanity he has been refused).

• Close analysis: "Devoted" (with its older meaning of consecrated to a fate) elevates the violence into ritual; "select specimen" is the language of natural science, the Creature using Victor's own scientific vocabulary against him; "irremediable" names the absolute irreversibility — neither Victor's death nor the Creature's death can repair the original wrong.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader recognises the symmetry: Victor's pursuit of the Creature across Europe and into the Arctic exactly mirrors the Creature's pursuit of Victor, and neither will outlive the chase.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader feels the Creature's grief over Victor's body — "I shall die, and what I now feel be no longer felt" — as the final recognition that revenge, having been completed, leaves no recipient to address.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be arguing that the form revenge takes is symmetrical destruction — the rejected pursues the rejecter, and only when the chase ends do both parties recognise that the original injury made the chase inevitable.

• Context (AO3): Aristotle's Poetics required tragedy to deliver catharsis (the audience's emotional release through pity and fear); Shelley delivers it not through Victor's death — which the audience may not mourn — but through the Creature's mourning of Victor, the antagonist grieving the protagonist who refused him.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, through Justine's hanging, the "everlasting war" vow, and the symmetrical Arctic pursuit, Shelley presents revenge as the form rejection takes when the rejected can no longer access any other relationship — a force that consumes both pursuer and pursued.

• Controlling concept: The novel's controlling argument is that creation without responsibility makes monsters — and the monstrosity, once made, expresses itself as revenge precisely because relationship has been refused.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be using the Miltonic-Satan lineage to argue that the figures Romantic culture sympathised with — the rejected, the outsider, the cursed — are also the figures most likely to embody revenge; sympathy and revenge are continuous on the same scale.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that the time to interrupt revenge is before it begins — at the moment of the original abandonment in Chapter 5, not at the moment Victor and the Creature meet across the Arctic ice.

Q9 — Sublime nature

Preamble

In this extract, Victor crosses the Mer de Glace above Chamonix and reflects on the sublime landscape before him.

Extract

Volume II Chapter 10 — Victor on the Mer de Glace above Chamonix:

"His tale had occupied the whole day, and the sun was upon the verge of the horizon when he departed. I knew that I ought to hasten my descent towards the valley, as I should soon be encompassed in darkness; but my heart was heavy, and my steps slow. The labour of winding among the little paths of the mountain and fixing my feet firmly as I advanced perplexed me, occupied as I was by the emotions which the occurrences of the day had produced. Night was far advanced when I came to the halfway resting-place and seated myself beside the fountain. The stars shone at intervals as the clouds passed from over them; the dark pines rose before me, and every here and there a broken tree lay on the ground; it was a scene of wonderful solemnity and stirred strange thoughts within me. I wept bitterly, and clasping my hands in agony, I exclaimed, "Oh! stars and clouds and winds, ye are all about to mock me; if ye really pity me, crush sensation and memory; let me become as nought; but if not, depart, depart, and leave me in darkness."

These were wild and miserable thoughts, but I cannot describe to you how the eternal twinkling of the stars weighed upon me and how I listened to every blast of wind as if it were a dull ugly siroc on its way to consume me.

Morning dawned before I arrived at the village of Chamounix; I took no rest, but returned immediately to Geneva. Even in my own heart I could give no expression to my sensations—they weighed on me with a mountain's weight and their excess destroyed my agony beneath them. Thus I returned home, and entering the house, presented myself to the family. My haggard and wild appearance awoke intense alarm, but I answered no question, scarcely did I speak. I felt as if I were placed under a ban—as if I had no right to claim their sympathies—as if never more might I enjoy companionship with them. Yet even thus I loved them to adoration; and to save them, I resolved to dedicate myself to my most abhorred task. The prospect of such an occupation made every other circumstance of existence pass before me like a dream, and that thought only had to me the reality of life."

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Shelley presents nature and the sublime in Frankenstein.

Write about:

- how Shelley presents nature in this extract

- how Shelley presents nature in the novel as a whole.

[30 marks]

Frame

Sublime nature in Frankenstein dwarfs human ambition. The Romantic-period sublime — vast mountains, polar ice, glaciers, storms — produces awe that contextualises Victor's pretensions to godlike creation. Mont Blanc and the Arctic make Victor small.

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• The sublime = the Romantic experience of awe in the face of vast, terrifying nature.

• Pathetic fallacy = nature reflecting human emotion (the "terrifically desolate" landscape matching Victor's grief).

• Visual imagery = imagery you can see ("icy and glittering peaks").

• Hyperbole = deliberate exaggeration ("in a thousand spots").

• Personification = giving non-human things human qualities ("my heart… swelled with something like joy" and the mountains' "aerial summits hung over its recesses").

• Romantic irony = the gap between a speaker's claim of mastery and the scene's evidence that they are small (Victor pretends to godlike creation; the glacier dwarfs him).

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Edmund Burke's A Philosophical Enquiry into the Origin of Our Ideas of the Sublime and Beautiful (1757) had distinguished the sublime — the experience of awe before vast, terrifying nature — from the beautiful, and the Romantic poets after him (Wordsworth, Coleridge, Byron, Percy Shelley) made the sublime central to their account of nature.

• Building sentence (historical context): Mary Shelley, who travelled through the Alps with Percy Shelley in 1816 (the same summer she began Frankenstein at Villa Diodati), set the novel's central confrontation on the Mer de Glace — the same glacier her husband would describe in Mont Blanc (1817) — making the sublime not a backdrop but the moral measuring-rod against which Victor's ambition is set.

• Thesis: Across the Mer de Glace setting in Volume II Chapter 10, the polar landscape of Walton's letters in Letters 1–4 and Chapter 24, and the Arctic catastrophe of the closing pages, Shelley uses sublime nature to dwarf human ambition — making Victor's pretensions to godlike creation visible as the small, doomed gestures they are.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning of the novel — 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: Walton's polar landscape (Letters 1–4)

• Topic: In the novel's opening, Shelley uses Walton's Arctic to establish that nature in Frankenstein is vast and indifferent — a force human ambition can encounter but never master.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "I shall satiate my ardent curiosity with the sight of a part of the world never before visited, and may tread a land never before imprinted by the foot of man" (Walton, Letter 1) combines hyperbole ("never before visited"; "never before imprinted" — totalising claims of pristine territory) with the abstract noun "curiosity" (Walton's drive named openly).

• Close analysis: "Satiate my ardent curiosity" uses the language of appetite — curiosity treated as a hunger to be fed; "never before imprinted by the foot of man" sets human presence against natural untouchedness, the Arctic ice imagined as a blank Walton will mark.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader feels the Romantic allure of the unknown — the same pull that drives Victor in Chapter 4 — and registers its danger.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader notes Walton's image of "foot of man" as foreshadowing — the Creature's enormous footprints in the snow at the novel's close will be the precise inversion of this opening claim.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be using Walton's polar ambition to establish from the novel's first letters that nature exceeds the human — the Arctic does not need Walton, and his expedition is a small wager against a vast blank.

• Context (AO3): Burke's account of the sublime (1757) had argued that terror was the precondition of the experience — Walton's Arctic provides exactly that terror, framed as opportunity by the Romantic appetite for the unknown.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle of the novel — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words; the printed extract usually sits here)

Scene focus: the Mer de Glace (extract, Volume II Chapter 10)

• Topic: At the novel's central encounter, Shelley uses the glacier and Mont Blanc to dwarf Victor's pretensions to godlike creation — the man who thought he could master life confronted by a landscape that has mastered him.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "Their icy and glittering peaks shone in the sunlight over the clouds. My heart, which was before sorrowful, now swelled with something like joy" combines visual imagery ("icy and glittering peaks"; "shone in the sunlight" — light on ice, the cliché of the Romantic sublime) with personification ("my heart… swelled" — Victor's emotion physicalised as bodily expansion in response to the landscape).

• Close analysis: "Icy and glittering" pairs the cold (deadly) with the radiant (beautiful) — the sublime's signature combination of terror and awe; "over the clouds" lifts the peaks above the human realm, the mountains literally above Victor's altitude; "swelled with something like joy" is the reflexive response, the protagonist responding to the landscape with the body before reason can intervene.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader experiences the Romantic sublime through Victor's eyes — the same combination of terror and exaltation Burke had named as constitutive.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader recognises the Romantic irony: Victor, who claimed to make life out of dead matter, is here visibly small compared to the glacier — and within seconds the Creature will appear and demand that Victor accept the responsibility he has refused.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be using the Mer de Glace to measure Victor against nature — the man who pretended to godhood revealed as the small figure on the ice, dwarfed by the landscape that produced him.

• Context (AO3): Percy Shelley's poem Mont Blanc (composed July 1816, the same summer his wife began Frankenstein) treated the same glacier as the figure for power without intent — vast, indifferent, beyond human moral category — and Mary Shelley's Mer de Glace shares the same philosophical inheritance.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End of the novel — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: the Arctic catastrophe (Volume III Chapter 24)

• Topic: At the novel's close, Shelley delivers the final triumph of nature — the polar ice as the landscape that consumes both Victor and the Creature, the only force in the novel larger than either.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "He sprung from the cabin window, as he said this, upon the ice-raft which lay close to the vessel. He was soon borne away by the waves and lost in darkness and distance" (Walton, on the Creature's exit, Chapter 24) combines passive construction ("was borne away" — the Creature acted upon by the ocean, not acting) with the alliterative pairing "darkness and distance" (the d-sounds binding the words into an absolute departure).

• Close analysis: "Sprung" is the verb of physical leap, the Creature's last act of agency; "borne away by the waves" immediately removes that agency, the Arctic taking him; "darkness and distance" turns the Creature into pure absence — no longer a being but a direction.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader feels the Arctic take the Creature into itself the way the glacier measured Victor — nature having the final, larger word in both confrontations.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader recognises the closing image as the correction of the novel's opening — Walton, who began Letter 1 hoping to imprint his foot on land never before visited, ends Chapter 24 turning his ship back, having seen the cost of the Romantic ambition the Arctic punishes.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be arguing that the only relationship human ambition can sustain with sublime nature is humility — Walton's turning-back is the moral alternative Victor refused.

• Context (AO3): The Romantic-period sublime — Burke 1757, Wordsworth's Prelude, Percy Shelley's Mont Blanc — gave Mary Shelley the philosophical apparatus to argue that nature exceeds the human; the Arctic ice closing over the Creature is that apparatus dramatised.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, through Walton's polar opening, the Mer de Glace confrontation, and the Arctic closing, Shelley uses sublime nature to dwarf human ambition — making Victor's pretensions to godlike creation visible as the small, doomed gestures they are.

• Controlling concept: The novel's controlling argument is that creation without responsibility makes monsters — and the measure of that monstrosity is the indifferent, vast nature that exceeds every human attempt to master it.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be writing the novel into the Romantic-period sublime — Burke's terror, Wordsworth's awe, Percy Shelley's Mont Blanc — using the same apparatus to argue that human science cannot master the natural world, only humble itself before it.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that the only sustainable relationship with sublime nature is humility — Walton's turning-back is the lesson Victor refused.

Q10 — The three-frame structure: how the form trains the reader

Preamble

In this extract, Walton opens his first letter to his sister Margaret Saville on setting out for the Arctic.

Extract

The opening of Walton's first letter to his sister Margaret Saville (Letter 1):

"You will rejoice to hear that no disaster has accompanied the commencement of an enterprise which you have regarded with such evil forebodings. I arrived here yesterday, and my first task is to assure my dear sister of my welfare and increasing confidence in the success of my undertaking.

I am already far north of London, and as I walk in the streets of Petersburgh, I feel a cold northern breeze play upon my cheeks, which braces my nerves and fills me with delight. Do you understand this feeling? This breeze, which has travelled from the regions towards which I am advancing, gives me a foretaste of those icy climes. Inspirited by this wind of promise, my daydreams become more fervent and vivid. I try in vain to be persuaded that the pole is the seat of frost and desolation; it ever presents itself to my imagination as the region of beauty and delight. There, Margaret, the sun is for ever visible, its broad disk just skirting the horizon and diffusing a perpetual splendour. There—for with your leave, my sister, I will put some trust in preceding navigators—there snow and frost are banished; and, sailing over a calm sea, we may be wafted to a land surpassing in wonders and in beauty every region hitherto discovered on the habitable globe. Its productions and features may be without example, as the phenomena of the heavenly bodies undoubtedly are in those undiscovered solitudes. What may not be expected in a country of eternal light? I may there discover the wondrous power which attracts the needle and may regulate a thousand celestial observations that require only this voyage to render their seeming eccentricities consistent for ever. I shall satiate my ardent curiosity with the sight of a part of the world never before visited, and may tread a land never before imprinted by the foot of man. These are my enticements, and they are sufficient to conquer all fear of danger or death and to induce me to commence this laborious voyage with the joy a child feels when he embarks in a little boat, with his holiday mates, on an expedition of discovery up his native river. But supposing all these conjectures to be false, you cannot contest the inestimable benefit which I shall confer on all mankind, to the last generation, by discovering a passage near the pole to those countries, to reach which at present so many months are requisite; or by ascertaining the secret of the magnet, which, if at all possible, can only be effected by an undertaking such as mine."

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Shelley uses the structure of Frankenstein to shape the reader's response.

Write about:

- how Shelley uses structure in this extract

- how Shelley uses structure in the novel as a whole.

[30 marks]

Frame

The novel's three-frame structure — Walton's letters → Victor's narrative → the Creature's narrative — is itself the argument. The form trains the reader to feel sympathy where Victor refuses it. By the time the Creature speaks for himself in Volume II, the reader has been prepared, by Walton's careful listening and Victor's slow remorse, to receive the Creature as a person speaking — not a monster being described.

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Epistolary form = a novel told through letters.

• Frame narrative = a story inside a story, where one narrator hands the telling to another.

• First-person narration = the speaker telling his own story ("I").

• Direct address = speaking to a named person ("Margaret").

• Confessional register = the speaker telling intimate truths to a known reader.

• Dramatic irony = where the reader knows something the speaker does not.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): The eighteenth-century English novel — Samuel Richardson's Pamela (1740) and Clarissa (1748) — had used the epistolary form to give readers privileged access to a single mind; the form fell from favour by 1800 but was revived for specific Gothic effects in the early nineteenth century.

• Building sentence (historical context): Mary Shelley, writing Frankenstein in 1816–17, deliberately revived the epistolary frame in a triple-nested form — Walton's letters → Victor's narrative → the Creature's narrative — drawing on Wollstonecraft's and Godwin's belief that moral sympathy is built only through patient attention to the voices of those society silences.

• Thesis: Across Walton's opening letters, Victor's middle narrative, and the Creature's voice in Volume II, Shelley uses the three-frame structure to train the reader in the moral attention the novel's characters cannot practise — by the time the Creature speaks for himself, the framing Victor first delivered as "miserable monster" has been comprehensively reversed.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning of the novel — 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: Walton's first letter (extract, Letter 1)

• Topic: In the novel's opening letters, Shelley establishes the outer frame — Walton's voice — as the listener-figure whose moral attention will model the reader's.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "You will rejoice to hear that no disaster has accompanied the commencement of an enterprise which you have regarded with such evil forebodings" combines direct address ("You will rejoice"; "my dear sister" — Walton speaking to a named, present-in-his-mind listener) with the confessional register (intimate disclosure of his sister's "evil forebodings" about his expedition).

• Close analysis: "You will rejoice" presumes a sympathetic listener already on Walton's side; "such evil forebodings" admits the listener has previously worried — the relationship between speaker and listener is one of mutual care, the model of attention the novel will later test.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader is positioned as Margaret — the careful listener whose forebodings have not yet been confirmed but may be — receiving the story from someone who needs to be heard.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader recognises that the form itself — letters, addressed to a known sympathetic listener — is already teaching the reader how to listen.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be using the epistolary opening to install the listener-mode in the reader before Victor speaks — so that when Victor narrates, the reader is already attuned to detect what Victor leaves out.

• Context (AO3): Wollstonecraft's argument that moral sympathy is built through patient attention to the voices of the marginalised directly informs the choice of opening; Shelley begins not with Victor but with the listener.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle of the novel — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words; the printed extract usually sits here)

Scene focus: Victor's narrative inside Walton's frame (Volume I)

• Topic: At the novel's middle frame, Shelley shows Victor's narration as the voice the reader must learn to read against — first-person but unreliable, present but evasive.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "Unable to endure the aspect of the being I had created, I rushed out of the room" combines first-person narration ("I had created"; "I rushed") with euphemism ("the being I had created" — clinical abstraction in place of naming the Creature).

• Close analysis: First-person narration claims authority — Victor speaking his own story; euphemism undermines that authority — Victor refusing to name what he is describing; the reader, trained by Walton's frame to listen carefully, is positioned to catch the gap between what Victor admits and what he refuses to say.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader feels the gap between Victor's first-person claims and the reality the language conceals — the framing structure has installed the suspicion the words now confirm.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader recognises that first-person does not mean reliable — and that the novel's form requires the reader to read with critical attention rather than passive trust.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be using the middle frame to test whether the reader has learned what Walton's opening taught — careful listening produces the doubt that Victor's narration confirms.

• Context (AO3): Godwin's Caleb Williams (1794) — written by Mary Shelley's father — had used first-person narration to dramatise an unreliable narrator's self-justification, the form's inheritance Shelley is borrowing for Victor.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End of the novel — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: the Creature's narrative — the inner frame (Volume II Chapters 11–16)

• Topic: At the novel's deepest frame, Shelley delivers the Creature's own voice — the most articulate narration in the novel, given the most marginalised body.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "It is with considerable difficulty that I remember the original era of my being; all the events of that period appear confused and indistinct" combines first-person narration ("I remember"; "my being") with the Romantic register ("the original era of my being" — a phrase that could be from a Wordsworth lyric, the Creature speaking as a Romantic poet).

• Close analysis: "Considerable difficulty" is the language of careful introspection; "the original era of my being" is metaphysical, philosophical — the Creature thinking about his own consciousness with the precision of a Romantic-period intellectual; the register itself contradicts every label Victor has applied to him.

• Effect on audience 1: The reader hears the Creature speak with a subtlety and self-awareness that the "miserable monster" label could not predict.

• Effect on audience 2: The reader recognises that the framing has been reversed — the man who narrated the middle frame (Victor) is less articulate than the "monster" who narrates the inner frame (the Creature), and the structural shape itself argues that Victor's framing was always wrong.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be arguing that the form of the novel — three frames, three voices, three opportunities to be heard — is itself the moral education the characters refuse: the reader meets the Creature with the careful attention Victor refused.

• Context (AO3): Wollstonecraft's argument that moral sympathy is built through patient attention finds its most extreme test in the inner frame — the most marginalised possible voice given the most articulate possible voice, and the reader trained by the form to receive both.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, through Walton's listener-frame, Victor's evasive middle narration, and the Creature's articulate inner voice, Shelley uses the three-frame structure to train the reader in the moral attention the novel's characters cannot practise.

• Controlling concept: The novel's controlling argument is that creation without responsibility makes monsters — and the three-frame structure is the form by which the novel teaches the reader to recognise what Victor refuses to recognise.

• Author's purpose: Shelley may be using the form itself as the lesson — sympathy is not a feeling to be persuaded, it is a practice to be trained, and the structure of the novel is the training.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that the form of a story is part of its argument — and the reader who emerges from Frankenstein able to hear the Creature is the reader the novel set out to make.

When you walk into the exam, twelve scenes are doing all the work. Memorise these once, with two embedded quotes per scene, and every AQA Frankenstein question becomes a recombination of the same corpus.

Three context factors that travel across all ten themes

1. Wollstonecraft (1792) + Godwin (1793) — circumstantial determinism + moral accountability — for Q1, Q3, Q4, Q7.

2. Galvanism (Galvani 1791, Aldini 1803) + 1816 vitalism debates — for Q2, Q4, Q6.

3. The Romantic sublime (Burke 1757, Wordsworth, Percy Shelley's Mont Blanc 1817) + Villa Diodati 1816 — for Q5, Q6, Q9, Q10.

Two critical voices that travel across all ten

• Anne Mellor — Mary Shelley: Her Life, Her Fiction, Her Monsters (1988). Reads Victor's failure as responsibility, not curiosity; distinguishes science that describes from science that controls.

• Maurice Hindle — Mary Shelley: Frankenstein (1994). Reads the novel as Mary Shelley's biographical inheritance from Wollstonecraft and Godwin, dramatising the family's intellectual project in fiction.

Use one context factor per body paragraph, threaded — never bolted on. Use one critical voice per essay, named, with year. The "three claims" of the controlling concept (creation without responsibility makes monsters / sympathy is the moral instrument institutions refuse / the three-frame structure trains the reader) should land at least once across the introduction-thesis or conclusion of every essay.
